heroOverview: Achondroplasia is the most common non-lethal skeletal dysplasia, affecting approximately 1 in 15,000 to 1 in 40,000 live births worldwide. It is caused in more than 98% of cases by a single recurrent point mutation (p.Gly380Arg) in FGFR3 — the fibroblast growth factor receptor 3 gene — which locks the receptor into a constitutively active state that suppresses bone elongation at the growth plates. The result is disproportionate short stature, with shortened limbs against a near-normal trunk, together with a characteristic enlarged head, midface hypoplasia, and lumbar lordosis. Intelligence is typically normal. The condition has broad clinical relevance because adults with achondroplasia face a range of preventable complications including spinal cord compression, sleep apnoea, and recurrent ear infections, and because new pharmacological therapies — most notably vosoritide — have recently been approved that measurably increase growth velocity in children.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 1 in 15,000–40,000 live births; estimated 250,000 people affected worldwide (Orphanet, 2023)
keyStats.demographics: Affects males and females equally; occurs in all racial and ethnic groups
keyStats.avgAge: Present from birth; diagnosed prenatally by ultrasound or clinically at birth in most cases
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 250,000 individuals living with achondroplasia globally

definition: Achondroplasia (ICD-10: Q77.4) is an autosomal dominant genetic disorder of endochondral ossification caused by gain-of-function mutations in FGFR3, with over 98% of cases attributable to the same nucleotide change (c.1138G>A or c.1138G>C, both producing p.Gly380Arg). The constitutively active FGFR3 receptor signals continuously through the RAS–MAPK pathway to inhibit chondrocyte proliferation and differentiation in growth plates, reducing longitudinal bone growth. The appendicular skeleton — the limbs — is most affected because it depends entirely on endochondral ossification, whereas the axial skeleton has additional membranous ossification. Key features include rhizomelic shortening (the most proximal limb segments are disproportionately short), trident hand, thoracolumbar kyphosis in infancy progressing to lumbar hyperlordosis, foramen magnum stenosis, and midface hypoplasia. Approximately 80% of cases arise from de novo mutations, most occurring on the paternal allele and correlating with advancing paternal age. The autosomal dominant pattern means an affected parent has a 50% chance of passing the condition to each child. The rare homozygous form (inheriting two mutant FGFR3 alleles) is lethal due to severe thoracic restriction. Achondroplasia is managed by clinical genetics, paediatric orthopaedics, and neurosurgery.

typesClassification.description: The standard survivable form; one mutant copy of FGFR3 alongside one normal copy. Adults reach an average height of about 130 cm (women) to 138 cm (men) and have near-normal intelligence and lifespan when complications are managed.

Around 80% of cases arise from a new mutation; neither parent is affected, so there is no prior family history. The specific mutation rate on the paternal allele increases measurably with advancing paternal age above 35–40 years.

When one or both parents have achondroplasia; if one parent is affected, 50% of children will inherit the condition. If both parents have achondroplasia, offspring have a 25% chance of homozygous achondroplasia (lethal), 50% heterozygous, and 25% unaffected.

Two copies of the FGFR3 p.Gly380Arg mutation; results in a far more severe phenotype with a narrow thorax incompatible with life, resembling thanatophoric dysplasia. Occurs only when both parents have achondroplasia.

primarySymptoms: Rhizomelic disproportionate short stature — the humerus and femur (proximal segments) are disproportionately short relative to the forearms, lower legs, and trunk, giving the characteristic short-limbed appearance.
Macrocephaly with frontal bossing — the head circumference is large, the forehead prominent, and the occiput flattened; this reflects relative restriction of the cranial base.
Midface hypoplasia with a depressed nasal bridge, which can contribute to airway narrowing and obstructive sleep apnoea.
Trident hand — a distinctive shape where the fingers tend to be short and diverge into three groups, with a gap between the third and fourth fingers.
Lumbar hyperlordosis (exaggerated inward curve of the lower back), which develops as toddlers begin to walk and is often accompanied by a protuberant abdomen.
Thoracolumbar kyphosis (a rounding at the chest-to-lumbar junction) in infancy, usually improving with sitting but potentially persisting to require orthopaedic management.
Genu varum (bowed legs) and tibial torsion, causing a characteristic gait pattern and occasional pain.
Restricted elbow extension — most people with achondroplasia cannot fully straighten their elbows.
Foramen magnum stenosis — the opening at the base of the skull is narrower than normal, potentially compressing the brainstem and upper cervical cord.
Obstructive sleep apnoea caused by midface hypoplasia, adenotonsillar hypertrophy, and central apnoea from foramen magnum compression.
Recurrent otitis media from eustachian tube dysfunction secondary to midface hypoplasia, often leading to conductive hearing loss if not treated.
earlyWarningSigns: Femur length on second-trimester ultrasound measuring more than two standard deviations below expected for gestational age.
A sudden change in growth velocity or limb-to-trunk ratio noted on serial pregnancy scans.
Hypotonia (floppy tone) in a newborn with the typical facial and limb appearance.
Snoring, restless sleep, or witnessed apnoea episodes in a young child with achondroplasia.
emergencySigns: Sudden or progressive weakness, numbness, or loss of bladder and bowel control — these can signal acute foramen magnum or spinal cord compression and require immediate neurosurgical assessment.
Central apnoea causing blue episodes or oxygen desaturation during sleep, especially in infants under 12 months.
Loss of developmental milestones (sitting, crawling, walking), particularly in the first two years, which may reflect neurological compromise from cervicomedullary compression.
Severe back pain with bilateral leg weakness in an adult, suggesting lumbar spinal stenosis causing cauda equina syndrome.

causes.description: A single nucleotide substitution at position 1138 of FGFR3 replaces glycine with arginine in the transmembrane domain, causing the receptor to signal continuously even without its growth factor ligand. Continuous FGFR3 signalling through the RAS–MAPK pathway strongly inhibits chondrocyte proliferation in the growth plate, reducing longitudinal bone elongation.

causes.cause: De novo mutation on paternal allele
causes.description: Approximately 80% of achondroplasia cases arise as new mutations, and the mutational event disproportionately occurs on the paternal chromosome. Sperm are produced continuously throughout life, accumulating replication errors; advancing paternal age (particularly above 35–40 years) measurably increases the rate of the specific FGFR3 mutation.
When a parent has achondroplasia, each gamete has a 50% chance of carrying the FGFR3 p.Gly380Arg allele. Each child therefore has a 50% probability of inheriting the condition.

When both parents have heterozygous achondroplasia, there is a 25% per-pregnancy probability that both copies of FGFR3 carry the mutation, producing the lethal homozygous phenotype resembling thanatophoric dysplasia.

A small minority of achondroplasia cases result from other FGFR3 missense mutations at different positions; these produce an identical or very similar clinical picture and are detectable only by sequencing the full FGFR3 gene.

riskFactors.description: A parent with achondroplasia has a 50% probability of transmitting the FGFR3 p.Gly380Arg allele to each child. Genetic counselling is recommended before or during pregnancy for affected individuals.

De novo mutations causing achondroplasia occur predominantly on the paternal allele. The rate of the specific FGFR3 mutation increases with paternal age; fathers over 40 have a measurably (though still absolutely small) higher likelihood of having an affected child.

Each pregnancy carries a 25% risk of lethal homozygous achondroplasia, 50% risk of heterozygous achondroplasia, and 25% probability of an unaffected child. Prenatal diagnosis is strongly recommended in this situation.
A family history of achondroplasia or related FGFR3 disorders alerts clinicians to the possibility of an affected pregnancy and prompts early referral for specialised prenatal ultrasound.

riskFactors.factor: No known modifiable or environmental triggers
riskFactors.description: Unlike teratogen-related malformations, achondroplasia results from a specific gene mutation; maternal diet, exposures, or medications have not been causally linked to its occurrence.

affectedDemographics: Male and female individuals equally across all ethnicities
Approximately 80% of cases are born to parents of average stature (de novo mutation)
Children of one or two affected parents carrying inherited FGFR3 mutations
Children of fathers over age 40, who have a slightly elevated de novo mutation rate
All live births regardless of geography or socioeconomic background
diagnosisOverview: Achondroplasia is frequently diagnosed prenatally from a second-trimester detailed ultrasound that shows limb shortening — particularly the femur and humerus — with a relatively large head; measurements typically fall more than two standard deviations below expected values by 22–24 weeks, though recognition can be earlier. The diagnosis is confirmed by molecular analysis of FGFR3 in fetal DNA obtained by chorionic villus sampling or amniocentesis. When not diagnosed prenatally, the characteristic phenotype at birth — rhizomelic short stature, macrocephaly with frontal bossing, depressed nasal bridge, and trident hand — is usually recognised by the neonatologist or paediatrician and confirmed by sequencing. Radiography of the skeleton demonstrates characteristic findings including a small, narrow foramen magnum on skull radiograph, short square iliac wings with horizontal acetabular roofs ('champagne glass' pelvis), and short tubular bones with metaphyseal flaring. FGFR3 molecular testing confirms the diagnosis in equivocal cases. After birth, comprehensive clinical assessment includes neurological evaluation for foramen magnum stenosis, sleep study (polysomnography), ear and hearing assessment, and orthopaedic examination. Brain and spine MRI is indicated in infants with hypotonia, developmental delay, or signs of cervicomedullary compression.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies short femur and humerus with macrocephaly; the primary prenatal screening tool that triggers further investigation.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A detailed anomaly scan at 18–22 weeks or earlier; a fetal medicine specialist measures all four limbs carefully and assesses head size and thoracic dimensions.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects the p.Gly380Arg mutation in fetal or postnatal DNA, confirming the diagnosis with near-100% sensitivity for classic achondroplasia.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw or cheek swab postnatally; prenatal sample from CVS or amniocentesis. Results typically available within one to three weeks.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates the characteristic radiographic pattern: short tubular bones, squared iliac wings, small foramen magnum, and 'bullet-nose' lumbar vertebral bodies.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A series of plain X-rays at birth or in infancy; painless and takes 15–20 minutes. No preparation needed.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates foramen magnum dimensions, cervicomedullary junction compression, and hydrocephalus in infants with hypotonia, developmental concerns, or abnormal neurological examination.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30–60 minute MRI scan; infants often require sedation for the procedure. No radiation involved.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects obstructive and central sleep apnoea, which is common in children and adults with achondroplasia and can require intervention.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An overnight study in a sleep laboratory where electrodes monitor breathing, oxygen levels, and sleep stages while the child sleeps.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies conductive hearing loss from recurrent otitis media and eustachian tube dysfunction, which is common from early childhood.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Age-appropriate hearing tests, including tympanometry to assess middle ear function; usually performed by an audiologist in clinic.

treatmentOverview: Management of achondroplasia is multidisciplinary and aimed at preventing and treating complications rather than correcting the underlying genetic cause. The approach spans neurosurgery (foramen magnum decompression when indicated), orthopaedic surgery (spinal decompression, limb-related procedures), ear-nose-throat care (grommets for recurrent otitis media), and sleep medicine (CPAP or adenotonsillectomy for sleep apnoea). In 2021, vosoritide — a C-type natriuretic peptide analogue that counters FGFR3 over-signalling — was approved in the EU and subsequently in other jurisdictions for children with open growth plates, and clinical trials showed an increase in annualised growth velocity of approximately 1.6 cm/year compared with placebo. Limb-lengthening surgery (distraction osteogenesis) is an option some individuals choose in adolescence or adulthood to increase height, though it is a major undertaking requiring months of external fixation. Growth hormone therapy has been studied but produces only modest and unsustained height gains and is not recommended as primary treatment. Regular multidisciplinary review — typically every six to twelve months in childhood — coordinates neurological surveillance, developmental assessment, and ongoing preventive care. Psychological support and connection to achondroplasia community organisations are integral to holistic management.

medicalTreatments.name: Vosoritide (CNP analogue)
medicalTreatments.description: A daily subcutaneous injection of a modified C-type natriuretic peptide that activates NPR-B to counteract FGFR3 over-signalling in growth plate chondrocytes. Approved for children with open growth plates in many jurisdictions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In pivotal Phase III trials, vosoritide increased annualised growth velocity by approximately 1.6 cm/year compared with placebo over 52 weeks, representing a clinically meaningful improvement in final height trajectory.

medicalTreatments.name: CPAP or BiPAP for sleep apnoea
medicalTreatments.description: Continuous or bilevel positive airway pressure delivered via a mask during sleep; used when polysomnography identifies significant obstructive or central apnoea not correctable by adenotonsillectomy alone.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Normalises oxygen saturation during sleep in approximately 70–80% of users with properly fitted equipment; requires regular mask size adjustments in growing children.

medicalTreatments.name: Grommets (tympanostomy tubes) for chronic otitis media
medicalTreatments.description: Small ventilation tubes inserted into the eardrum under brief general anaesthesia to equalise middle ear pressure and drain fluid, preventing hearing loss from recurrent ear infections.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Resolves effusion in roughly 85–90% of cases and prevents conductive hearing loss while tubes remain in place.

medicalTreatments.name: Surveillance neurology protocol
medicalTreatments.description: Structured periodic MRI and neurological examination in infancy and early childhood to detect foramen magnum stenosis before irreversible cord injury occurs; the natural history data support early intervention in those with compression.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Published cohort studies show that prophylactic foramen magnum decompression in neurologically asymptomatic but radiologically compromised infants prevents neurological deterioration in approximately 90% of cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Orthopaedic bracing for kyphosis and genu varum
medicalTreatments.description: Thoracolumbar kyphosis in infancy may be managed with a custom-fitted orthosis to maintain spinal alignment; genu varum in toddlers is observed and treated only if progressive or painful.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Kyphosis bracing prevents progression to fixed deformity in a significant proportion of affected children when started in the first two years; spontaneous resolution of genu varum occurs in approximately 50% without surgery.

surgicalOptions.name: Foramen magnum decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Neurosurgical enlargement of the foramen magnum to relieve cervicomedullary compression; indicated for infants with hypotonia, developmental delay, apnoea, or MRI evidence of cord signal change.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Neurological stabilisation or improvement in approximately 85–95% of cases when performed by experienced paediatric neurosurgeons before irreversible cord damage.

surgicalOptions.name: Lumbar spinal decompression
surgicalOptions.description: Laminectomy or laminotomy to decompress the lumbar spinal canal in adults with symptomatic stenosis causing neurogenic claudication or leg weakness; the narrow spinal canal in achondroplasia predisposes to stenosis from mid-adulthood.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptom relief in approximately 70–80% of patients; recurrence requiring repeat surgery occurs in about 20–30% over 10 years.

surgicalOptions.name: Limb-lengthening (distraction osteogenesis)
surgicalOptions.description: A bone is surgically cut (osteotomy) and a fixator device slowly distracts the ends apart at 1 mm per day, allowing new bone to form in the gap. Can lengthen the femur, tibia, and humerus in staged procedures, potentially adding 30–40 cm total height over several years.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mean height gain of 30–35 cm achievable; significant complication rates including pin-site infection (30–40%), joint contracture, and nerve injury require experienced centres and dedicated rehabilitation.

surgicalOptions.name: Adenotonsillectomy for obstructive sleep apnoea
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of enlarged tonsils and adenoids to widen the upper airway; often the first-line surgical intervention for obstructive sleep apnoea in children with achondroplasia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Resolves or significantly reduces obstructive apnoea in approximately 60–70% of children with achondroplasia; supplemental CPAP may still be needed postoperatively.

alternativeTreatments.name: Physiotherapy and motor development support
alternativeTreatments.description: Early physiotherapy optimises developmental milestones, strengthens trunk and neck muscles to support the large head, and addresses gait abnormalities from genu varum and hyperlordosis.

alternativeTreatments.name: Occupational therapy and adaptive equipment
alternativeTreatments.description: Occupational therapists assess and provide adaptations — extended light switches, modified seating, step stools — that enable full independence in home, school, and workplace environments.

alternativeTreatments.name: Community and peer support organisations
alternativeTreatments.description: Organisations such as the Restricted Growth Association (UK) and Little People of America connect individuals and families, provide practical guidance, and advocate for access and inclusion.

whySeeSpecialist: A clinical geneticist should be involved at diagnosis to provide accurate recurrence risk figures and molecular confirmation. Paediatric neurosurgery review in the first year of life is recommended for all infants because foramen magnum stenosis can be neurologically silent until it is severe. Orthopaedics, ENT, sleep medicine, and audiology input ensures a comprehensive surveillance programme. Adults benefit from specialist spine surgeons experienced in achondroplasia because standard lumbar decompression techniques need to be adapted to the anatomy.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for a multidisciplinary skeletal dysplasia clinic where a clinical geneticist, paediatric neurosurgeon, paediatric orthopaedic surgeon, ENT surgeon, and sleep physician collaborate. In the UK, NHS Highly Specialised Services designate specific centres. Ask about their experience with foramen magnum decompression in infants, access to polysomnography services, and familiarity with vosoritide prescribing for eligible children. For limb-lengthening, choose a centre that performs at least 20–30 cases annually with published outcome data.
preventionStrategies: Genetic counselling for affected individuals or carrier couples before pregnancy to understand transmission risks and available prenatal options.
Prenatal diagnosis by FGFR3 molecular testing from CVS or amniocentesis when a parent has achondroplasia or a previous affected child.
Preimplantation genetic testing (PGT-M) for affected couples choosing IVF, which can select unaffected embryos or avoid homozygous-affected embryos in couples where both parents are affected.
There is no intervention to prevent de novo mutations in the general population.
Newborn screening protocols in specialised centres ensure that all key complications — foramen magnum stenosis, hearing loss, sleep apnoea — are identified and monitored from birth.
lifestyleModifications: Avoid activities carrying significant risk of neck hyperextension or axial spinal loading (trampolining, collision sports, diving) until foramen magnum status has been formally assessed.
Use supportive seating and appropriate car seats in infancy that support the large head and prevent chin-to-chest hyperkyphosis.
Maintain a healthy body weight, as obesity exacerbates joint pain, back pain, and sleep apnoea beyond the baseline risk.
Perform regular low-impact exercise such as swimming or cycling to maintain cardiovascular fitness and muscle strength without high axial joint loading.
Install home adaptations (grab rails, modified worktops, reachable light switches) to support independence and reduce falls risk.
Attend regular scheduled reviews even when feeling well, as spinal stenosis and sleep apnoea can worsen gradually without obvious symptoms.

recommended: Balanced diet with adequate calcium and vitamin D to support bone health throughout life
Caloric intake appropriate to actual height and activity level — standard adult calorie targets may lead to excess weight in people of short stature
High-fibre foods to support bowel regularity, which can be affected by spinal lordosis and reduced mobility during flares
avoid: Caloric excess that leads to obesity, as every excess kilogram substantially increases load on the lumbar spine and worsens stenosis symptoms
Heavy alcohol consumption, which increases fall risk and bone fragility

exerciseGuidelines: Low-impact aerobic activities — swimming, cycling, walking on even surfaces — are encouraged and beneficial. High-impact activities (running on hard surfaces, jumping, contact sports) and those involving axial compression or neck hyperextension should be cleared by the treating neurosurgeon before participation. Children with achondroplasia should be encouraged to be physically active within safe parameters; physiotherapy can advise on suitable exercises for each developmental stage. Adults with symptomatic spinal stenosis should work with a physiotherapist to identify a tolerable exercise programme that does not aggravate neurogenic claudication.
dailyManagement: Keep track of any new neurological symptoms — tingling, weakness, or bladder or bowel changes — and report them promptly to the neurology team.
Monitor sleep quality; report snoring, witnessed apnoea, or excessive daytime sleepiness to the sleep medicine team.
Attend annual audiology review to detect any progressive hearing loss.
Ensure school and work environments have appropriate adaptations; occupational therapy assessments can be requested through healthcare or education services.
Take all prescribed medications (including vosoritide, if prescribed) consistently and report injection-site reactions or side effects to the prescribing team.
Connect with patient advocacy organisations for peer support and updated information on new therapies.
prognosis: Life expectancy for people with heterozygous achondroplasia is near-normal when complications are identified and managed. The most preventable cause of early death is cervicomedullary compression in infancy; structured surveillance and timely surgical decompression have substantially reduced this risk over the past two decades. Adults face progressive lumbar spinal stenosis, which typically becomes symptomatic in the fourth and fifth decades and usually responds well to surgical decompression. Cardiovascular and metabolic risks are increased in the presence of obesity, which is more prevalent in this population. With appropriate orthopaedic, neurological, and medical follow-up, the majority of people with achondroplasia live independently, pursue careers across all fields, and enjoy a full quality of life. Vosoritide and other treatments in development offer the prospect of meaningfully increasing final adult height when started in children with open growth plates.
recoveryTimeline: After foramen magnum decompression in infants, neurological improvement (if present) typically begins within weeks, with full functional recovery over one to six months depending on the degree of pre-surgical compression. After lumbar spinal decompression in adults, most patients experience significant symptom relief within the first four to six weeks of recovery; full recovery takes three to six months. Limb-lengthening programmes span twelve to twenty-four months per surgical stage, involving a period of distraction followed by consolidation before the fixator is removed. For vosoritide, height velocity improvements are seen from the first year of treatment and are expected to continue while growth plates remain open.
complications: Cervicomedullary compression from foramen magnum stenosis — the leading cause of sudden infant death and developmental regression in young children if undetected.
Obstructive and central sleep apnoea causing hypoxia, pulmonary hypertension, and cognitive impairment if untreated.
Lumbar spinal stenosis in adulthood causing neurogenic claudication, leg pain, and eventually weakness or bowel and bladder dysfunction.
Conductive hearing loss from chronic otitis media, which can impair speech and language development if not managed with grommets and audiological support.
Obesity, which amplifies joint pain, sleep apnoea, and spinal stenosis beyond the baseline risks inherent to achondroplasia.
Psychological and social challenges including low self-esteem, bullying in childhood, and barriers to employment and physical access — addressable with psychological support and advocacy.

supportResources.name: Little People of America
supportResources.description: U.S.-based community organisation providing peer support, medical resources, and advocacy for people of short stature and their families.

supportResources.name: Restricted Growth Association UK
supportResources.description: UK charity offering peer support, information on benefits and adaptations, and a network for individuals with restricted growth including achondroplasia.

supportResources.name: NIH MedlinePlus — Achondroplasia
supportResources.description: Plain-English genetics summary from the U.S. National Library of Medicine including gene information and management links.

sources.title: Ornitz DM, Legeai-Mallet L. Achondroplasia: Development, pathogenesis, and therapy. Dev Dyn, 2017
Hoover-Fong J et al. Lifetime impact of achondroplasia: Current evidence and perspectives on the natural history. Bone, 2021
Savarirayan R et al. Vosoritide for achondroplasia (ACH-0001 Phase III trial). N Engl J Med, 2020
NICE Guidance on achondroplasia and skeletal dysplasia services — NHS England
Orphanet — Achondroplasia prevalence and clinical summary

confusedWithConditions.name: Achondrogenesis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Achondrogenesis is uniformly lethal with extreme underdevelopment of the entire skeleton; achondroplasia is survivable with near-normal intelligence and lifespan.

confusedWithConditions.name: Diastrophic dysplasia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Diastrophic dysplasia (SLC26A2 mutations) also causes short-limbed stature but is distinguished by severe joint contractures, hitchhiker thumb, and cauliflower ear deformity not seen in achondroplasia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Osteogenesis imperfecta
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Osteogenesis imperfecta causes short stature through bone fragility and fractures from collagen defects, not growth plate inhibition; blue sclerae and fracture history differentiate it from achondroplasia.

relatedConditions.name: Achondrogenesis
relatedConditions.relevance: A lethal skeletal dysplasia at the severe end of the endochondral ossification failure spectrum, providing important contrast to the survivable achondroplasia phenotype.

relatedConditions.name: Chondrodysplasia punctata
relatedConditions.relevance: Another genetic chondrodysplasia with short-limbed stature and growth plate disruption, sharing the genetics clinic differential for infants with rhizomelic shortening.

relatedConditions.name: Osteogenesis imperfecta
relatedConditions.relevance: Enters the differential in cases of short stature with bone abnormalities; both are managed in multidisciplinary skeletal dysplasia clinics.

relatedConditions.name: Osteoporosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Adults with achondroplasia are at elevated risk of accelerated bone loss from reduced mobility and altered spinal mechanics; bone density monitoring is part of long-term care.

faqs.question: What is achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: Achondroplasia is the most common form of disproportionate short stature, caused by a specific mutation in the FGFR3 gene that slows cartilage growth at the growth plates. Arms and legs are short relative to the trunk, intelligence is normal, and most people live near-normal lifespans with appropriate medical care.

faqs.question: What causes achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: More than 98% of cases are caused by a single mutation — p.Gly380Arg in FGFR3 — that makes the growth factor receptor permanently active, blocking bone elongation at the growth plates. About 80% of cases arise as a new spontaneous mutation with no family history; the remaining 20% are inherited from an affected parent.

faqs.question: Is achondroplasia hereditary?
faqs.answer: Yes, in an autosomal dominant pattern. An affected parent has a 50% chance of passing the condition to each child. If both parents have achondroplasia, there is a 25% risk of a lethal homozygous form per pregnancy. About 80% of cases arise from a new mutation and cannot be anticipated from family history.

faqs.question: How is achondroplasia diagnosed?
faqs.answer: It is frequently detected on second-trimester prenatal ultrasound showing short limbs and a large head. Confirmation uses molecular testing of FGFR3. After birth, the clinical appearance together with a skeletal radiographic survey and gene test confirms the diagnosis in the vast majority of cases.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy with achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: Life expectancy is near-normal when complications are identified and managed. The main risk in infancy is cervicomedullary compression from a narrow foramen magnum; adults face spinal stenosis. With appropriate surveillance and treatment, most people with achondroplasia live full and independent lives.

faqs.question: What is vosoritide and who is it for?
faqs.answer: Vosoritide is a daily injection that counters the over-active FGFR3 receptor by mimicking a natural signalling molecule called C-type natriuretic peptide. It is approved for children with achondroplasia who still have open growth plates. Clinical trials showed it increases height gain by about 1.6 cm per year compared with placebo.

faqs.question: Does achondroplasia affect intelligence?
faqs.answer: No. Intelligence is typically normal in achondroplasia. However, untreated cervicomedullary compression in infancy can cause developmental delay, so neurological surveillance in the first two years of life is important to protect cognitive outcomes.

faqs.question: What is foramen magnum stenosis and how common is it?
faqs.answer: The foramen magnum is the opening at the base of the skull through which the spinal cord passes. In achondroplasia it is narrower than normal and can compress the brainstem and upper cord. MRI studies suggest a significant proportion of infants have radiological compression; symptomatic cases require surgical decompression.

faqs.question: Can achondroplasia be detected before birth?
faqs.answer: Yes. Shortened limbs — particularly the upper arm and thigh bones — are visible on second-trimester ultrasound, usually from about 20–24 weeks. FGFR3 molecular testing from a chorionic villus sample or amniocentesis confirms the diagnosis before birth.

faqs.question: What surgeries might someone with achondroplasia need?
faqs.answer: Common surgeries include foramen magnum decompression in infancy for cervicomedullary compression, grommets for recurrent ear infections, adenotonsillectomy for sleep apnoea, and lumbar spinal decompression in adulthood. Some individuals choose limb-lengthening procedures; these are elective and require careful multidisciplinary discussion.

faqs.question: Is limb-lengthening surgery recommended?
faqs.answer: Limb-lengthening is available but is not universally recommended; it is a major multi-year undertaking with meaningful complication rates. Many achondroplasia advocacy organisations emphasise quality of life and acceptance over height, and the decision is personal. Centres performing this surgery should have specialist experience and provide realistic outcome data.

faqs.question: Can both parents have achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: Yes. When both parents have achondroplasia, each pregnancy has a 25% chance of homozygous achondroplasia (lethal), 50% chance of heterozygous achondroplasia, and 25% chance of average stature. Prenatal diagnosis and genetic counselling are strongly recommended before and during pregnancy for this couple.

faqs.question: Why do people with achondroplasia get recurrent ear infections?
faqs.answer: Midface hypoplasia shortens and distorts the eustachian tube that drains the middle ear. This impairs drainage, predisposing to fluid accumulation and infection. Grommets (ventilation tubes) and adenoid removal can reduce this substantially, and regular hearing tests ensure any conductive loss is identified early.

faqs.question: Does achondroplasia cause pain?
faqs.answer: Many adults experience back pain from lumbar hyperlordosis and spinal stenosis, and joint pain from altered gait mechanics. These are manageable with physiotherapy, weight control, pain medication, and, when necessary, spinal surgery. Identifying and treating the specific cause of pain — rather than accepting it as inevitable — improves quality of life significantly.

faqs.question: What precautions should children with achondroplasia take in sport?
faqs.answer: Activities involving forceful neck extension, trampolining, high-impact jumping, or collision sports should be cleared by a neurosurgeon before participation because of foramen magnum and cervical spine vulnerability. Swimming and cycling are generally well tolerated. A physiotherapist can advise on safe sport choices for each individual.

faqs.question: Can someone with achondroplasia have an unaffected child?
faqs.answer: Yes. An affected parent has a 50% chance with each pregnancy of having a child who does not inherit the FGFR3 mutation and will be of average stature. Prenatal genetic testing can confirm or exclude the mutation in the fetus if parents wish to know before birth.

faqs.question: Is preimplantation genetic testing (PGT) available?
faqs.answer: Yes. Couples where one or both partners have achondroplasia can use IVF with preimplantation genetic testing to identify embryos that are unaffected or to avoid homozygous-affected embryos. This is particularly important for couples where both parents have achondroplasia given the 25% risk of a lethal homozygous pregnancy.

faqs.question: What support resources exist for people with achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: Little People of America and the Restricted Growth Association (UK) are the main peer-support organisations. They offer connection to others with lived experience, guidance on adaptations and rights, and updates on new treatments. NHS and genetics clinics should also provide access to social work and psychological support.

faqs.question: How often should adults with achondroplasia see a specialist?
faqs.answer: Annual review with a specialist familiar with the condition is generally recommended to screen for emerging spinal stenosis, monitor sleep, and assess general health. Those with established spinal stenosis or other complications may need more frequent assessment.

faqs.question: Are there other drugs in development for achondroplasia?
faqs.answer: Several agents targeting FGFR3 or downstream pathways are in clinical trials, including infigratinib (an oral FGFR3 inhibitor) and burosumab in related conditions. The field is advancing rapidly. The prescribing geneticist or paediatrician is the best source of current trial eligibility information.

metaDescription: Achondroplasia is the most common skeletal dysplasia, causing short limbs from an FGFR3 mutation. Learn symptoms, diagnosis, vosoritide, and complication care.
